summary: Dan Luu reviews Steve Yegge's 2004 tech predictions, finding them remarkably accurate despite seeming obvious in hindsight. Most predictions were directionally correct, including the rise of JavaScript, cloud hosting, NoSQL databases, and mobile computing, with only the Lisp prediction missing significantly. The analysis demonstrates how good predictions suffer from hindsight bias—they seem obvious after the fact but were controversial when made—and shows how Yegge underestimated market inertia in his timing but correctly identified major industry trends.
key_points: Steve Yegge made 10 tech predictions in 2004 that were largely accurate in direction if not exact timing
Most misses were due to underestimating market inertia and the effect of 'dark matter' programmers
Good predictions appear obvious in hindsight due to hindsight bias, making them seem less impressive than they were
Yegge correctly predicted JavaScript's rise, cloud hosting dominance, NoSQL adoption, and mobile's importance years before they happened
The only major miss was predicting Lisp would break into top 10 languages by 2010
money_quotes: It's a no-win proposition: if you're right, hindsight bias makes it look like you're pointing out the obvious. And most predictions are wrong.
The misses are mostly caused by underestimating the sheer about of inertia it takes for a young/new solution to take over.
There's a massive amount of dark matter -- programmers who do business logic and don't blog or give talks -- that makes this prediction unlikely to come true in the near future.
Of course these all these things seem obvious in retrospect, but that's just part of Steve's plan to pass as a normal human being.
key_insight: Accurate predictions about technology trends suffer from a paradox: hindsight bias makes them seem obvious after the fact, obscuring how controversial and non-obvious they were when originally made, while the main failure mode is underestimating how long market inertia delays even correct directional insights.
